text: This is Dr. Craig Rogers, Director of Renal Surgery at the Vatakuta Urology Institute at Henry Ford Hospital. In this video, we demonstrate steps for bowel mobilization.

The surgeon incises the white line of Toldt with curved monopolar scissors, reflecting the bowel using sharp and blunt dissection techniques. The bowel is then repositioned, revealing the avascular plane developed between the posterior mesocolon and Gerota's fascia.

The fourth robotic arm grasps the anterior Girotis fascia, retracting the kidney anteriorly to aid in bowel mobilization. The surgeon delicately dissects the brighter yellow mesenteric fat from the paler fat of the Girotis fascia.
The robotic grasping instrument is used to spread open the space between the mesentery and Girota's fascia, facilitating incision of the tissue while protecting underlying structures.

The surgeon exposes the upper pole of the kidney by mobilizing the spleen or liver. The splenic flexure, pancreas, and splenic attachments are released to enhance the exposure of the renal hilum. The scissors are opened and used like a hook to carefully avoid trauma to the pancreas and splenic vessels.
The surgeon releases the splenic attachments, reflecting the spleen medially while carefully avoiding injury to the stomach, which is positioned behind the spleen.

The assistant uses a long tip suction to provide counter traction, aiding in the release of upper pole attachments of the kidney. For right-sided tumors, passive liver retraction is performed using a locking grasper inserted through a five millimeter subxiphoid port, securing the liver to the diaphragm.
In the next video, we demonstrate the identification of important anatomical landmarks while performing robotic partial nephrectomy.
